comments.body: In scene 1, the nostalgia is good. Makes you want her to linger there in the village and approach to the large town, but not venture in.
In 3, this is a great sentence: In the shallows of the ruts, old snow had been ground into black ice, slick where shadow lingered.
I felt like I was drifting a bit in scene 4. Not sure why though. I liked the journey and understood that it has changed.

Enjoyed first chapter. Feel you met your objectives in your notes.
Attention was focussed mostly in the opening section in the inn and the conversation with Issren at the end.
Concentration drifted in the market scenes. I got distracted by the boy stealing the ribbon. Why did a boy steal a ribbon? For giggles? To give to someone else? I got distracted by those questions and had to re read the subsequent paragraphs because I was distracted by that.

author_reply.body: Thank you for this. It's really useful. All feedback is helpful.

comments.body: Scene 3? I got a bit lost in the initial long description of the inn. Not sure it's all necessary, or could be simplified a bit and woven more into her movements?

Found it easy to follow the different scenes in this chapter. I'm enjoying the story and followed that it was memories.
Agree that Tamsin came across as business like whilst seris is remembering kissing Tamsin snaps her back to the present. Wondering how the collapse links back to why seris has returned….

Sorry forgot to give this feedback before and just remembered. In your notes for the section it says the story is with seris- but as a reader you don't actually find that out until towards the end of the scene. It wasn't clear if it's seris or Tamsin.
Would Tamsin 'grab' a rug from the chair to cover Dara if they are keeping things nice and calm in the room? Perhaps she 'picked' or 'plucked' it instead. A quieter action.

author_reply.body: I'll keep an eye on the name. Different genres so probably ok. Publisher will have a say

comments.body:
The forgery- fits with everything else- linking to the "something bigger"
Interested by Tamsin's refusal of Thorek. Clearly beef there- wondering if it's linked to seris leaving.
The watchers- their deaths surprised me- seemed out of place violence from Tamsin and seris. Why does watching deserve death without questioning? Is there something I've missed in understanding background of that?

author_reply.body:
You haven't missed anything — that scene was written quickly and needs more work. The characters' response is wholly in keeping with the threat level, but I haven't signposted that threat sufficiently. I need to establish the danger more clearly beforehand, and then have the watchers turn on Seris before Tamsin acts. That should make the violence feel inevitable rather than surprising.
Comments like this is why I need Beta Readers. I am really grateful for your effort.

comments.body:
Issrens rooms made me question what I had read in previous chapter. I had interpreted the previous description that his office was an organised chaos- he would know where things are but no-one else. Now reading this chapter it sounds more OCD like and more logical.
Thoreks recruitment- leaves me wondering why he would want to be involved? Why not just leave them to it? Still left wanting to know what happened.
The discussion with seris and solenne, I guess they are talking about thorek and some sort of prophecy or rather lack of…. "It's not written"

The glass going untouched. Does he have a drink problem? Solenne knows about it but issren doesn't? That's why issren provided the drink but solenne discouraged drinking it.
The fight scene read well and I followed it. Again for me, it was a bit stab first ask questions later. It reminded me of in Austin powers where the henchmen have families. Maybe they would find out what's going on if they interrogated someone. They seem quite set on violence as the first option which seems a bit incongruous to the opening chapters. Again wondering if I missed something in why they are such a threat.
Solenne's actions based on opening chapters I would have thought she was easing them into the afterlife and being kind … but now I'm wondering if the whole group is actually more sinister and she is taking their energy or killing them more definitely so they can't recover and tell others about the attack.

author_reply.body:
Hilary, three really strong reads here.
On the glass — you've read that exactly right. All three characters as intended, Thorek has been dealing with problems, and drink is a solution. Solenne is now his keeper, and Issren does not know him. Well done.
On the fight scenes — you haven't missed anything, and this is really useful feedback. The characters' responses are correct for my writing of the threat level, but I clearly haven't established that threat level for the reader sufficiently beforehand. I'm going to add a scene from Tamsin's point of view that lays out the ruthlessness of these smugglers before the violence arrives. That should make the response feel like professional necessity rather than a first instinct.
On Solenne — you're reading her exactly as I intend. She may be genuinely compassionate, but she is a priestess of a Death God, and that carries its own inevitability. Keep both of those readings in your head. You'll need them.
My signals to the reader here seem to be working as intended (mostly), because you are questioning things in your mind. I write with an intention that the reader puzzles over a charcter as more is revealed. Hopefully, by the end of the book, the reader thinks, "yes, that makes sense, real people are so much more complex than many heroes are written." My challenge is to make each additional reveal of character to be understandable but challenging and not make a reader think "That doesn't make sense with what I already know"

Hilary, I owe you an apology on this one. There are version control errors in what you've been given to read — two drafts visible where there should be one, and possibly a missing scene as a result. The confusion you felt at the end is entirely understandable and is my fault, not a reflection of the chapter.
I'll clean this up and repost the correct version. Please hold your conclusions on this chapter until you've had a chance to read the right draft.
On Seris — her character is being revealed over time, and what she is thinking will, at times, be at odds with what the reader can see clearly. That gap is intentional. By the end, you may find yourself wanting to grab her by the shoulders and shout 'Open your eyes!' That's exactly where I need you to be.
More soon, once I've sorted the version.

comments.body:
The interrogation scene was a quick and easy read. Made me realise this isn't about the smugglers they are interested in seris and what she can do. This is reinforced at the end of the chapter where they have arranged for them to escape and then pretend to follow.
Skirt registered- the colour of it didn't until I went back to check.
Wondering if Tamsin understands what they want with seris? Why she wasn't interviewed as well.

author_reply.body:
Hilary, three good reads here.
On the interrogation — you've understood exactly what that scene is doing. It isn't about the smugglers at all. Also, I didn't wish for a high threat interrogation. The Woman is wanting to understand, the man is wanting to trick a confession out of Seris. The Woman is in charge.
On the skirt — if it registered even subconsciously, it's doing its job. You'll need that detail later.
On Tamsin not being interviewed — you're asking exactly the right question. She wasn't interviewed because she had nothing they wanted, and someone knew that precisely. Sit with that. Tamsin feels the slight too, even if she can't name the source of it yet. I'll have a re-read and see if I have signalled this enough.

comments.body:
I enjoyed this chapter for a few different reasons. This chapter felt peaceful after the tension of the previous chapters. The sailing I loved. I grew up as a sailor and it really brought a lot of that back for me. The depth testing with a lead line, the terminology- mizzen mast, aft, gybe. I felt at home with all of it.
I also enjoyed the opening scene with the relationship between issren and bashirak. The way issren is acting as mentor and coach. I wish someone had taught me to be more observant like that my husband always says I don't notice things, maybe I need some coaching off issren.
To answer your questions- opening felt good. Peaceful and needed calm after the tension of previous chapters.
The sailing scenes felt very clear to me and I could really imagine myself there.
I did pick up on issren vouching for her with her family rather than herself. Interesting! clearly issren is respected by Kelaro. But I think issren has covered seris' involvement by broadening to her family. Will be interesting to see if her family crop up again.

Things I liked about this chapter- waiting for the gossip to give away something useful.
The punting description resonated for me with my attempts at punting in Cambridge and in France… although I always ended up with horrible blisters on my hands the next day! Tamsin must have tougher skin than me.
The red skirts! Does that mean the woman who interrogated her is her mum? Seems too much of a coincidence! Very invested in this story.
Frustrated my phone battery is dying on this bus so I will have to read more later rather than now. Safe to say I'm hooked into the story.

author_reply.body:
Hilary,
Delighted the punting resonated — and yes, Tamsin probably has considerably tougher hands than most!
On the red skirt — you're asking interesting questions. Keep reading.
Alastair

comments.body:
Hauling bags? I missed this earlier. Did issren sort these for them? What's in the bags? Remembered them being given a big bag of coins.
Attention grabbed at mention of valebrooks again. Aedith says the elves are haughty, I think when you introduced Tamsin you said she was elven. Showing a bit of local biases to those from 'away' despite claiming she isn't. Learn that seris' father looked after the district when they lived here.
For the water. It felt unnaturally cold. Either magic or water coming from somewhere else. Seeing similar movements with the land as in the town. Something big is going on. The fake smugglers have been coming here too.

author_reply.body:
Hilary,
The bags — when they land from the Baghla, Seris goes into the village for supplies. I don't think I specifically mentioned she put supplies in bags — easy fix!
On Ædith — you've caught something there. People rarely hear their own assumptions.
On the water — you're thinking like Issren. Keep pulling that thread.
Alastair

comments.body:
Red skirt lady- she is manipulating seris (and tamsin) curious to know what she wants and how she has turned her back on seris. Seris didn't remember her mother but this lady knows what she would do as a child and the sorts of decisions she would make so has she been watching from afar?
The lattice disturbance being the same here as in alderwic was logical. I had reached that conclusion in the previous chapter so it acted to confirm it.
The watching man- hmm is he lurking up a corridor for her to go to bed. Does he have a message for her?

author_reply.body:
Hilary,
On the red skirt lady — you're building an interesting theory. Keep reading.
On the lattice disturbance confirming what you already suspected — good. That's exactly how it should feel at this point.
On the watching man — he's not there by accident.
Alastair

comments.body: Was someone watching her the night before? Have I just forgotten this?

I missed that she had recognised the watcher in the previous chapter. She knows who rendric is when aedith tells her he is dead.
I did pick up on seris reflecting on the escape and that Tamsin had noted it as well. They are starting to feel played but have still fallen into what red skirted woman wanted them to do.
Mention of a stepmother in this chapter.
I was getting hangry following this and enacted them to eat the pie. I was relieved they got an hour to eat and rest before they spotted a search party.
I liked the revenue man scene it felt clever.

author_reply.body:
Hilary,
The stepmother is just background detail. No thread to pull there.
On Rendric — you're right that the connection to the watcher in the previous chapter is easy to miss. I'll have a look at whether I've signalled it clearly enough.
On being played — yes. And they know it, which makes it worse.
Glad the revenue man landed well. And I'm delighted the pie was sufficiently convincing, and their condition made you hangry — this was my intention. I like a reader feeling the narrative.
Alastair

comments.body: I enjoyed this chapter. It was easy to follow each character update and follow their stories. Almost like a web, or a map with them all chatting new unexpected courses.

author_reply.body: Thanks Becca — that's a lovely way to put it. That's exactly what this chapter is meant to feel like.

comments.body:
Solenne is limited in her actions by what the temple requires of her. She has been finding ways to play the system to do what she wants for her friends and what she considers the right course of action.
The royal family are directing the red skirts woman. They have worked out she let S&T escape but haven't punished her directly. More they are seeing what she is on to… but I do think the being sent away is a punishment- she wasn't planning on it and she certainly didn't want to be replaced whilst she was away.

author_reply.body:
Hilary,
On Solenne — exactly right. She is a woman who knows the rules precisely well enough to work around them when she needs to.
On the red skirt woman — you're reading the power dynamic correctly. The sending away is absolutely a punishment, and she knows it. Whether it comes from the crown or another source entirely.
Alastair

comments.body:
I enjoyed the clever slight of hand from the tea seller.
The moment was unclear at the time, as it should have been, but then explained soon after.

author_reply.body: Thanks Becca — glad the tea seller worked. That slight delay before the penny drops is exactly what I was going for.

comments.body: Vauclare is confident, he doesn't care that issren knows what he is up to. He acts like someone above the law who doesn't fear anything.

author_reply.body:
Hilary,
Above the law, yes. That is a good way of putting it.
Alastair

comments.body: Issrens response to Vauclaire is great. I hope there is a response/revenge coming later.

author_reply.body: It would be nice to see Vauclaire get his comeuppance. Perhaps in the next book?

comments.body: An enjoyable chapter which was vivid in my imagination with how you described it. The whole chapter read at a good pace. I like thorek and solenne as characters and they seem like a good team.

author_reply.body:
Hilary,
Really glad this one landed well. Thorek and Solenne are a good team — you'll see more of that.
Alastair

comments.body:
The bargaining scene is fun.
This is a good sentence "Perhaps a month, perhaps less. Maybe more, but not three, I think."
I would enjoy trading in these ways!

author_reply.body: So would I. I loved this scene

comments.body:
I didn't have any issue with the misdirection. Just felt like part of the world building and frustrations of the group. I enjoyed this chapter too and it was a fast read. Scene 7 was a little slower for me as I tried to work out who said what.
Thoreks counsel by solenne felt reasonable.

author_reply.body: Thanks Hilary — really glad the misdirection felt like world-building rather than a stumble, and great to hear it was a fast read. Noted on scene 7 — I'll keep an eye on that.

comments.body: The grey mother was a beast and a hard battle. Alayne knew what it was. She was able to take care of herself she fired the arrow at it. She didn't want him there and had wanted to travel alone hard to tell if he was a help or a hindrance to her. I feel like she doesn't mind his company.

author_reply.body:
Hi Hilary,
An interesting working theory. This is the first time the name Alayne has appeared.
Alastair

[Second reply, 21:44]: Hilary,
I'm interested that you feel Alayne doesn't mind Berringer's company.
It was a hard battle. Was it easy enough to follow? Whether she could have handled The Grey Mother on her own or not, is not revealed at any point. It's left for the reader to surmise.
Kind regards
Alastair

comments.body:
I like thorek. He seems humble and aware of his shortcomings. He cares for solenne but also knows she is powerful and he respects her.
I found the description of the lattice useful. Explains why it's called a lattice. When I read this scene I remembered about the presence when dara was being looked at and I wondered again what that was.

author_reply.body:
Hi Hilary,
I have purposely been drip feeding the Lattice Structure over the course of the book. I did not wish to dump Lattice Lore early. I'd be interested to know if you feel you are receiving it in time, or too late. That is quite a challenge to decide as the author.
I'm pleased you like Thorek. He's grounded, I think, a solid established character with flaws.
Kind regards
Alastair

comments.body: I think the lattice lore is good timing.

The method of creating unease in the village worked well.
"Not settled"- not able to fully relax or rest. With the weird behaviour of the villagers that made sense to me.
The lattice is regular and balanced. I was expecting it to be more weird after the villager behaviour; but then she didn't feel the disturbance. Again mind wandered to the presence felt when Dara was being looked at.
Stone of lost names imagining it like one of the callanish standing stones.

author_reply.body:
Hi Hilary,
The Callanish Standing Stones - That's a good picture. I may steal it and update my descriptions in chapter 27.
Your mind wandering back to Dara. That's interesting. I like that.
The unease in the village, for me, is a clannish celtic village, very stand-off'ish to strangers. That's the feel I'm wanting to portray.
Kind regards
Alastair

comments.body: Ahh ok, I was reading lattice-y behaviour into the villagers rather than "we're not talking to offcomers" vibes. I was overthinking it.

Left with a feeling at the end of the chapter like the two women are weary.
Seris' decision to go to the tower- I felt it was risky of her and selfish to her friend who was exhausted along with the horses. Seris is singleminded and a bit selfish in that moment. Putting her own needs and curiosity above Tamsin's and the horses.
So my thoughts that seris is being singleminded are confirmed in this chapter but there is something bigger going on with Tamsin and not just tiredness that's why solenne wants seris to talk to her.

author_reply.body:
Hilary,
The weariness — yes. They've earned it by this point.
On Seris at the tower — you're not wrong. Sit with that feeling. Does it change how you see her compared to when you first met her in Chapter 1, and if so, how?
Alastair
